schools.summary: Gill: he had his name of Gileadite from the city and country where he was born; a mighty man of valour, the son of a harlot.

themes: Now Jephthah the Gileadite was a mighty man of valour, and he was the son of an harlot
God raising his servants from the despised and base, whatever their birth
God hath chosen the base things of the world, and things which are despised (1 Cor. 1:28)

schools.summary: Gill: Gilead took a lawful wife after Jephthah's birth, who bore him sons; they said, Thou shalt not inherit, for thou art the son of a strange woman.

themes: they thrust out Jephthah... Thou shalt not inherit in our father's house; for thou art the son of a strange woman
the chosen of God despised and cast out by his own
he came unto his own, and his own received him not (John 1:11)

schools.summary: Gill: he could not bear to be treated with contempt nor live in dependence, and so sought to make his own way; vain men gathered to him.

themes: Jephthah fled from his brethren, and dwelt in the land of Tob: and there were gathered vain men to Jephthah
the rejected outcast become the rallying-point of the needy
every one that was in distress... gathered themselves unto him (1 Sam. 22:2)

schools.summary: Gill: this was at the close of the eighteen years' oppression by Ammon; the children of Ammon made war against Israel.

themes: And it came to pass in process of time, that the children of Ammon made war against Israel
God using the troubles of his people to bring them to repentance and the saviour
before I was afflicted I went astray: but now have I kept thy word (Ps. 119:67)

schools.summary: Gill: the war being begun and pressing upon them.
Keil & Delitzsch: the elders going to fetch Jephthah from Tob.

themes: the elders of Gilead went to fetch Jephthah out of the land of Tob
the proud humbled to seek in distress the help they once refused
they shall look upon me whom they have pierced (Zech. 12:10)

schools.summary: Gill: they proposed him as general, not as king, being warned by Abimelech's conduct; to fight with Ammon.

themes: Come, and be our captain, that we may fight with the children of Ammon
the rejected outcast lifted to the place of command
the stone which the builders refused is become the head of the corner (Ps. 118:22)

schools.summary: Gill: he was not backward to engage, but thought it proper to upbraid them with their former unkindness.
Clarke: his reproach, why come ye now in your distress?

themes: Did not ye hate me, and expel me out of my father's house? and why are ye come unto me now when ye are in distress?
the wronged justly remembering the wrong, yet ready to help
in their affliction they will seek me early (Hos. 5:15)

schools.summary: Gill: their reply to his objection.
Keil & Delitzsch: them offering him to be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead.

themes: Therefore we turn again to thee now... and fight against the children of Ammon, and be our head over all the inhabitants of Gilead
God lifting the lowest and disinherited to the highest place
whosoever shall humble himself shall be exalted (Matt. 23:12)

schools.summary: Gill: considering their former usage and the fickleness of men, he was willing to make a sure bargain; if the LORD deliver them before me.

themes: If ye bring me home again to fight... and the LORD deliver them before me, shall I be your head?
prudence joined with piety, resting the victory wholly on the LORD
except the LORD build the house, they labour in vain that build it (Ps. 127:1)

schools.summary: Gill: they assented, giving not only their word but their oath.
Keil & Delitzsch: their words, The LORD be witness between us.

themes: The LORD be witness between us, if we do not so according to thy words
the sacredness of a promise made and witnessed in God's name
the LORD watch between me and thee, when we are absent one from another (Gen. 31:49)

schools.summary: Gill: he went from Tob into Gilead, his native country.
Keil & Delitzsch: the people making him head, and he uttered his words before the LORD.

themes: the people made him head and captain over them: and Jephthah uttered all his words before the LORD in Mizpeh
the godly leader undertaking his charge in dependence on God
commit thy works unto the LORD, and thy thoughts shall be established (Prov. 16:3)

schools.summary: Gill: being now chief governor, he acted in character and sent messengers to the king of Ammon.

themes: What hast thou to do with me, that thou art come against me to fight in my land?
seeking a peaceable understanding before resorting to the sword
if it be possible... live peaceably with all men (Rom. 12:18)

schools.summary: Gill: the king returned this answer, that Israel had taken his land; he demanded the lands restored peaceably.

themes: Because Israel took away my land, when they came up out of Egypt... now therefore restore those lands again peaceably
the unjust cloaking their greed with a false show of right
a false witness that speaketh lies (Prov. 6:19)

schools.summary: Gill: he was willing to give all satisfaction, and if possible live peaceably and prevent bloodshed.
Keil & Delitzsch: him sending again.

themes: And Jephthah sent messengers again unto the king of the children of Ammon
the godly slow to war and earnest for peace, sparing no pains
blessed are the peacemakers: for they shall be called the children of God (Matt. 5:9)

schools.summary: Gill: he answers by his messengers.
Keil & Delitzsch: the denial, Israel took not the land of Moab nor of Ammon.

themes: Thus saith Jephthah, Israel took not away the land of Moab, nor the land of the children of Ammon
the truth set against the false claim, the best defence of a righteous cause
the lip of truth shall be established for ever (Prov. 12:19)

schools.summary: Gill: they came up from Egypt, which lay low, to go to Canaan, which was higher.
Ellicott: compares the narrative with Numbers.

themes: when Israel came up from Egypt, and walked through the wilderness unto the Red sea, and came to Kadesh
grounding the plea in the true history of God's dealings
I will remember the works of the LORD: surely I will remember thy wonders of old (Ps. 77:11)

schools.summary: Gill: refers to the history of the request to Edom.
Keil & Delitzsch: the king of Edom not hearkening, so Israel abode in Kadesh.

themes: Then Israel sent messengers unto the king of Edom... but the king of Edom would not hearken... so Israel abode in Kadesh
the people of God seeking leave courteously even from the unkind
let your moderation be known unto all men (Phil. 4:5)

schools.summary: Gill: the wilderness of Paran along the borders of Edom, going round on the south; they came not within the border of Moab.

themes: compassed the land of Edom, and the land of Moab... but came not within the border of Moab
the people of God respecting others' rights, rendering each his due
provide things honest in the sight of all men (Rom. 12:17)

schools.summary: Gill: Heshbon was his royal city, which he had taken from the Moabites; Israel asked leave to pass through his land.

themes: And Israel sent messengers unto Sihon king of the Amorites... Let us pass, we pray thee, through thy land into my place
the consistent peaceableness of God's people, offering fair terms first
he... desireth conditions of peace (Luke 14:32)

schools.summary: Gill: Sihon feared they would seize his dominions, being one of the Canaanite nations doomed to destruction; he gathered his people and fought against Israel.

themes: Sihon gathered all his people together, and pitched in Jahaz, and fought against Israel
the guilt of conflict lying with him who refuses peace and attacks
they hated me without a cause (Ps. 69:4)

schools.summary: Gill: through the victory God gave them over Sihon, Israel had a divine right to the land.
Keil & Delitzsch: Israel possessing all the land of the Amorites.

themes: the LORD God of Israel delivered Sihon and all his people into the hand of Israel... so Israel possessed all the land of the Amorites
the land God's gift through victory, held by his grant not by wrong
the heaven... is the LORD's: but the earth hath he given to the children of men (Ps. 115:16)

schools.summary: Gill: they possessed it peaceably, none disputing their title or giving disturbance.
Clarke: the bounds from the wilderness to Jordan.

themes: they possessed all the coasts of the Amorites, from Arnon even unto Jabbok, and from the wilderness even unto Jordan
the long undisturbed possession a strong title under God's hand
he hath... appointed the bounds of their habitation (Acts 17:26)

schools.summary: Gill: it is God's doing, not the work of Israel, that dispossessed the Amorites; shouldest thou possess it?

themes: the LORD God of Israel hath dispossessed the Amorites from before his people Israel, and shouldest thou possess it?
the title resting on God's own deed; none may take what he bestows
what therefore God hath joined together, let not man put asunder (Mark 10:9)

schools.summary: Gill: Chemosh was the idol of the Moabites, leading some to think this king ruled Moab also; whomsoever the LORD drives out, Israel will possess.

themes: Wilt not thou possess that which Chemosh thy god giveth thee... So whomsoever the LORD our God shall drive out... will we possess
the people of God holding their inheritance by the gift of the living God
the LORD killeth, and maketh alive... he bringeth low, and lifteth up (1 Sam. 2:6-7)

schools.summary: Gill: this strengthens the conjecture that the king was king of Moab, Balak being his predecessor; Balak never strove or fought against Israel for the land.

themes: art thou any thing better than Balak the son of Zippor, king of Moab? did he ever strive against Israel?
a long-acknowledged right not overturned by a later challenger
remove not the ancient landmark, which thy fathers have set (Prov. 22:28)

schools.summary: Gill: Heshbon was the principal city, taken from Moab by Sihon, then by Israel; Israel dwelt there three hundred years.

themes: While Israel dwelt in Heshbon and her towns... three hundred years? why therefore did ye not recover them within that time?
right long enjoyed and never disputed stands firm against late claims
the truth of the LORD endureth for ever (Ps. 117:2)

schools.summary: Gill: he had done him no injury, nor taken any part of his country, speaking in the name of all Israel.
Clarke: he calls the LORD the Judge to judge between them.

themes: the LORD the Judge be judge this day between the children of Israel and the children of Ammon
committing the cause to the LORD who judges justly
shall not the Judge of all the earth do right? (Gen. 18:25)

schools.summary: Gill: he attended not to the arguments, nor chose to seem convinced, nor regarded the appeal.

themes: the king of the children of Ammon hearkened not unto the words of Jephthah which he sent him
the obstinacy of the aggressor deaf to reason, truth, and right
they refused to hearken, and pulled away the shoulder, and stopped their ears (Zech. 7:11)

schools.summary: Gill: the spirit of strength, fortitude, valour, and zeal for God and Israel.

themes: Then the Spirit of the LORD came upon Jephthah, and he passed over Gilead, and Manasseh... unto the children of Ammon
all true might for God's work coming from his Spirit
not by might, nor by power, but by my spirit, saith the LORD (Zech. 4:6)

schools.summary: Gill: he vowed before setting out, hoping such a religious disposition would be acceptable.

themes: And Jephthah vowed a vow unto the LORD, and said, If thou shalt without fail deliver the children of Ammon into mine hands
vows to God to be made with care and wisdom, not in rashness
be not rash with thy mouth... when thou vowest a vow unto God, defer not to pay it (Eccl. 5:2-4)

schools.summary: Gill: if 'to meet me' is meant intentionally, no other than a human creature can be meant, a child or servant; he would offer it for a burnt offering.

debates: The witnesses note the ambiguity of 'whatsoever cometh forth to meet me': Gill holds that what comes 'to meet' him must be a human creature, making the vow rash and its issue dreadful; the full question of whether his daughter was slain or devoted to perpetual virginity is weighed at verses 39-40.
themes: whatsoever cometh forth of the doors of my house to meet me... shall surely be the LORD's, and I will offer it up for a burnt offering
the peril of binding oneself to God in haste, without weighing the cost
it is a snare to the man... and after vows to make enquiry (Prov. 20:25)

schools.summary: Gill: he passed over after he had made the vow.
Ellicott: the clause records the result, the LORD delivering them.

themes: So Jephthah passed over unto the children of Ammon to fight against them; and the LORD delivered them into his hands
God making good his help and giving the victory to those who trust him
thou hast also given me the necks of mine enemies (Ps. 18:40)

schools.summary: Gill: Aroer, near the river Arnon, on the borders of Moab.
Keil & Delitzsch: him smiting twenty cities with a great slaughter.

themes: he smote them from Aroer... even twenty cities... with a very great slaughter. Thus the children of Ammon were subdued
the abundance of the deliverance God wrought, the oppressor laid low
he saved them from the hand of him that hated them (Ps. 106:10)

schools.summary: Gill: she was the first person to present to his view, at the head of the virgins with timbrels; she was his only child.

themes: his daughter came out to meet him with timbrels and with dances: and she was his only child
the rash vow meeting its dreadful fulfilment in the dearest
it is a snare to the man... and after vows to make enquiry (Prov. 20:25)

schools.summary: Gill: she was the first to present to his view as she led the virgins.
Keil & Delitzsch: his cry, I have opened my mouth unto the LORD, and cannot go back.

themes: Alas, my daughter!... for I have opened my mouth unto the LORD, and I cannot go back
vows to God binding, yet a rash vow binding the conscience to sorrow
better is it that thou shouldest not vow, than that thou shouldest vow and not pay (Eccl. 5:4-5)

schools.summary: Gill: the conditional 'if' may be omitted; she said, do to me according to thy vow, since the LORD hath avenged thee.

themes: do to me according to that which hath proceeded out of thy mouth; forasmuch as the LORD hath taken vengeance for thee
faith setting God's honour above her own life, a noble submission
not my will, but thine, be done (Luke 22:42)

schools.summary: Gill: she had but one favour to ask, which might be granted without breach of the vow.
Keil & Delitzsch: her request to bewail her virginity.

themes: let me alone two months, that I may go up and down upon the mountains, and bewail my virginity, I and my fellows
her lamenting her virginity bearing on the meaning of the vow; meek acceptance
the cup which my Father hath given me, shall I not drink it? (John 18:11)

schools.summary: Gill: he granted her request at once; she bewailed her virginity upon the mountains with her companions.

themes: And he said, Go... and she went with her companions, and bewailed her virginity upon the mountains
facing the hardest trial with seriousness and faith, not fleeing nor murmuring
in your patience possess ye your souls (Luke 21:19)

schools.summary: Gill: her request was no pretence to escape, but she returned at the time fixed; her father did according to his vow, and she knew no man.

debates: Was Jephthah's daughter slain or devoted to virginity?

The witnesses present both readings: the plain words of the vow ('offer it up for a burnt offering') suggest she was sacrificed, while the note that 'she knew no man,' the bewailing of her virginity, and the yearly commemoration (not mourning of death) lead many to hold that she was devoted to perpetual virginity and the LORD's service. Gill leaves the question open; both views are held among interpreters.
themes: her father... did with her according to his vow which he had vowed: and she knew no man
the seriousness of a bond made to God and the danger of a rash vow
when thou vowest a vow unto God... pay that which thou hast vowed (Eccl. 5:4)

schools.summary: Gill: either to lament her death, as some, or her virginity, as others; the word may signify to talk and discourse with her, a yearly confabulation.

themes: the daughters of Israel went yearly to lament the daughter of Jephthah the Gileadite four days in a year
the submission of God's people not forgotten but held in honour
the righteous shall be in everlasting remembrance (Ps. 112:6)
